status.state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
status.product_description: Fleet management telematics platform with GPS tracking, driver behavior scoring, fuel optimization, predictive maintenance, and ELD compliance.

plan.description: Define product requirements, roadmap, and success metrics for the fleet_telematics platform covering GPS tracking, driver behavior scoring, fuel optimization, predictive maintenance, and ELD compliance.
acceptance_criteria: PRD approved by stakeholders with all five feature areas scoped
Success metrics defined and measurable
Roadmap with phased milestones (MVP → GA) documented

plan.description: Document business requirements, fleet operator user stories, driver workflows, and ROI model for fuel savings and maintenance cost reduction.
acceptance_criteria: User stories cover all five product feature areas with acceptance criteria
Process flows validated with at least two fleet operator personas
ROI model shows ≥ 12-month payback period calculation

plan.description: Competitive analysis of Samsara, Verizon Connect, Motive (KeepTruckin), and Geotab. Identify differentiation opportunities in AI-driven fuel optimization and predictive maintenance.
acceptance_criteria: Feature comparison matrix covering all five competitors
Three or more differentiation opportunities identified with evidence
Pricing benchmarks documented

plan.description: Map all applicable regulatory requirements: FMCSA ELD mandate (49 CFR Part 395), UNECE R155/R156 (vehicle cybersecurity), ISO 26262 (functional safety), and AUTOSAR compliance checklist.
acceptance_criteria: Traceability matrix maps every regulatory clause to a product requirement
ELD technical specifications checklist complete (FMCSA self-certification checklist)
UNECE R155 cybersecurity threat catalog populated
ISO 26262 ASIL level assigned per vehicle function

plan.description: Perform system-level FMEA and hazard analysis for the telematics ECU and connected vehicle systems. Assign ASIL levels per ISO 26262. Produce fault tree for GPS spoofing, ECU lockup, and false maintenance alerts.
acceptance_criteria: FMEA covers all hardware and software failure modes with severity/occurrence/detection ratings
ASIL levels assigned for all safety-relevant functions (at minimum ASIL A for HOS alerts, ASIL B for brake event detection)
Fault trees drawn for top five hazard scenarios
Safety goals documented and traceable to system requirements
ASIL level determined for each function
Fault tree analysis complete

plan.description: Perform threat modeling (STRIDE) for the telematics platform per UNECE R155/R156. Cover OTA update pipeline, CAN bus injection, cloud API exposure, and driver data privacy.
acceptance_criteria: STRIDE analysis covers all six attack surfaces
CSMS (Cybersecurity Management System) policy document produced per UNECE R155
OTA signing key management procedure defined
SBOM generated covering all ECU firmware dependencies

plan.description: Design fleet manager dashboard, driver mobile app, and compliance officer reporting UI. Wireframes for GPS live map, driver scorecard, fuel analytics, maintenance queue, and ELD log viewer.
acceptance_criteria: Wireframes cover all seven screen types
Driver mobile app flow covers: login → view HOS → sign log → view score
Accessibility audit passes WCAG 2.1 AA for fleet portal
Design reviewed and approved by at least one fleet operator persona

plan.description: Create hardware simulation models for the telematics ECU: CAN bus simulation (Vector CANoe / CAPL scripts), GPS signal injection test bench, OBD-II diagnostic simulation, and accelerometer behavior model for driver scoring.
acceptance_criteria: CAN bus simulation produces valid J1939 and OBD-II PID messages at 250/500 kbps
GPS simulator generates NMEA 0183 sentences with configurable spoofing scenarios
Accelerometer model outputs configurable g-force profiles for harsh event injection
All four test scenarios produce deterministic, repeatable outputs

plan.description: Implement telematics ECU firmware: RTOS task architecture (FreeRTOS), HAL drivers for GPS (UART/NMEA), CAN bus (SocketCAN/MCP2515), OBD-II reader, accelerometer (SPI/I2C), LTE modem (PPP/MQTT), and secure OTA bootloader.
acceptance_criteria: All RTOS tasks boot without deadlock; watchdog recovery tested
GPS task parses NMEA GGA/RMC at 10Hz and buffers with < 100ms latency
CAN task decodes J1939 PGNs: fuel rate (PGN 65266), engine speed (PGN 61444), odometer (PGN 65248)
OTA bootloader verifies firmware signature (ECDSA-256) before flashing
AUTOSAR BSW layer mapping documented for OS, COM, and DIAG modules
AUTOSAR compliance checked
MISRA C violations zero

plan.description: Implement ELD compliance module in firmware: FMCSA-certified HOS logging, tamper detection, driver ID via NFC/PIN, data transfer via USB/BT/Telematics (3 mandatory methods), and unidentified driving detection.
acceptance_criteria: HOS log records all seven duty status changes with UTC timestamp and location
Three FMCSA data transfer methods functional and tested
Tamper events logged immutably with GPS coordinates and timestamp
Unidentified driving detection triggers within 30 seconds of motion without driver login
ELD output file validates against FMCSA ELD file format specification
AUTOSAR compliance checked
MISRA C violations zero

plan.description: Design time-series and relational database schema for fleet_telematics: vehicle telemetry (TimescaleDB/InfluxDB), trip records, driver events, maintenance schedules, ELD logs, and fuel transactions.

schemas: vehicles (id, vin, make, model, year, fleet_id)
telemetry (vehicle_id, ts, lat, lon, speed, heading, altitude, hdop)
driver_events (id, vehicle_id, driver_id, event_type, severity, ts, lat, lon, g_force)
trips (id, vehicle_id, driver_id, start_ts, end_ts, distance_km, fuel_used_L, score)
eld_records (id, driver_id, vehicle_id, duty_status, start_ts, end_ts, location, transfer_hash)
maintenance_schedules (id, vehicle_id, component, last_service_ts, next_due_km, predicted_failure_ts)
fuel_transactions (id, vehicle_id, ts, liters, cost, odometer)

acceptance_criteria: TimescaleDB hypertable for telemetry ingests 1000 rows/sec with < 10ms write latency
Geospatial queries (vehicles within bounding box) return in < 200ms on 1M row dataset
All foreign key relationships enforced with cascade rules defined
Migrations written and reversible via Alembic
ELD records table enforces immutability via append-only policy (no UPDATE/DELETE)

plan.description: Set up project infrastructure: monorepo structure, Docker Compose for local dev (TimescaleDB, Redis, MQTT broker, backend, frontend), GitHub Actions CI, environment configs, and MQTT topic schema for telematics ingestion.

repo_structure.frontend/: React dashboard + React Native driver app
repo_structure.ml/: Predictive maintenance and driver scoring models

payload.ci: GitHub Actions — lint, test, build firmware, docker build

acceptance_criteria: docker compose up starts all services with no manual intervention
MQTT broker accepts messages on all defined topic patterns
GitHub Actions CI pipeline runs on every PR and blocks merge on failure
Environment config uses .env files with no secrets in source control

plan.description: Implement telemetry ingestion service: MQTT subscriber consuming GPS/CAN/event payloads, validation pipeline, time-series write to TimescaleDB, real-time position broadcasting via WebSocket, and geofence evaluation engine.

features: MQTT message validation with Pydantic schemas
WebSocket broadcaster per fleet for live map updates
Geofence engine: polygon and radius, entry/exit event generation
Store-and-forward handling for offline device reconnection

acceptance_criteria: Ingestion service sustains 10,000 msg/sec with < 500ms end-to-end latency
Invalid messages rejected with error logged; valid messages persisted with 100% durability
WebSocket broadcasts vehicle positions to subscribed fleet dashboards within 1 second
Geofence violations generate alert events within 2 seconds of boundary crossing
Reconnecting device flushes store-and-forward buffer without data loss

plan.description: Build driver behavior scoring model: feature engineering from acceleration, braking, cornering, speeding, phone use events; supervised model trained on labeled fleet data; score normalization (0–100); per-trip and rolling 30-day scores.

payload.training_data: synthetic_fleet_dataset + publicly available SHRP2 naturalistic driving data
payload.serving: FastAPI ML endpoint, pre-computed per trip, cached in Redis

acceptance_criteria: Model achieves AUC > 0.82 on held-out test set predicting accident risk quartile
SHAP values expose top contributing factors per driver score
Score computed within 5 seconds of trip completion
Scoring endpoint returns p99 latency < 100ms from Redis cache
Score normalization produces uniform distribution across synthetic fleet dataset

plan.description: Build predictive maintenance model: multi-variate time-series anomaly detection on engine RPM, coolant temp, oil pressure, DTC frequency, and mileage since service. Predict component failure 2–4 weeks ahead.

payload.algorithm: Isolation Forest + LSTM for time-series patterns
payload.retraining: weekly on new fleet data via automated pipeline

acceptance_criteria: Precision ≥ 0.75 and recall ≥ 0.70 on brake pad failure prediction (30-day horizon)
False positive rate < 15% across all component types
Predictions updated daily per vehicle
Model version tracked in MLflow with experiment comparison
Retraining pipeline completes without manual intervention

plan.description: Build fuel optimization engine: per-vehicle fuel consumption baseline modeling, idle time analysis, route efficiency scoring, and eco-driving recommendations using trip and CAN bus data.

payload.benchmarking: compare against vehicle EPA rating + fleet historical baseline

acceptance_criteria: Fuel consumption prediction within 5% MAPE vs actual fuel card transactions
Idle detection accuracy > 95% vs GPS/speed ground truth
Eco-driving recommendations actionable (max 5 per trip, prioritized by impact)
Fleet-level fuel cost projection available in dashboard with 30-day forecast

plan.description: Implement core REST API services: fleet management CRUD, driver management, trip API, alert management, ELD compliance API, reporting endpoints, and webhook outbound notifications.
acceptance_criteria: All CRUD endpoints return correct HTTP status codes with Pydantic-validated responses
ELD log export endpoint produces FMCSA-compliant file format (UTF-8, tab-delimited)
JWT auth rejects expired tokens; API key scoped to fleet_id

plan.description: Build driver mobile app (React Native): HOS clock display, duty status selector, pre-trip inspection checklist, trip history with score, ELD log review and sign-off, and co-driver management.

screens: Dashboard — HOS clock, current status, violations
Duty Status — status change with location confirmation
Trip History — list with score badge, tap for breakdown
ELD Log — daily grid view, sign & certify flow
Pre-trip Inspection — DVIR checklist with photo capture
Co-driver — swap driver, unidentified drive assignment

acceptance_criteria: HOS clock accurate to within 1 second, updates without app refresh
Duty status change persists offline and syncs when connectivity restored
ELD log sign-off generates cryptographic signature and uploads to backend
DVIR checklist supports photo capture with offline storage
App passes Apple App Store and Google Play policy review requirements

plan.description: Implement multi-agent coordinator pattern for fleet intelligence: coordinator routes to specialist agents (driver coaching, maintenance planner, compliance monitor, fuel advisor). Each agent uses ReAct loops against fleet data tools.

agents.driver_coach: analyzes score decline → generates personalized coaching tips → proposes manager notification
agents.maintenance_planner: queries prediction model → checks shop availability → generates work order proposal
agents.compliance_monitor: scans HOS violations → generates corrective action → flags for compliance officer review
agents.fuel_advisor: identifies high-idle vehicles → generates route recommendations → estimates savings

payload.trigger: scheduled (hourly batch) + event-driven (trip completion, DTC event)

acceptance_criteria: Coordinator correctly routes trip completion events to driver_coach and fuel_advisor in parallel
Each agent produces a structured proposal with confidence score and supporting evidence
HITL gates fire for work orders and corrective actions before any external notification
Agent proposals stored in proposal_store with trace_id and audit trail
ReAct loops terminate within 10 seconds per agent with valid output or explicit failure

plan.description: Provision production infrastructure: Kubernetes cluster (EKS/GKE), TimescaleDB managed (Timescale Cloud or self-hosted), Redis Cluster, MQTT broker (HiveMQ or EMQ X), CDN for dashboard, VPN for device connectivity.

payload.cloud: AWS (primary), multi-region for ELD compliance data residency
networking: AWS IoT Core certificates for device MQTT auth
payload.data_residency: ELD records stored in US region only (FMCSA requirement)

acceptance_criteria: Terraform plan applies without errors; state stored in S3 backend
EKS cluster autoscales from 3 to 20 nodes based on CPU/memory
TimescaleDB achieves 99.9% uptime SLA with automated backups every 6 hours
ELD data confirmed stored only in us-east-1 / us-west-2
MQTT broker handles 50,000 concurrent device connections

plan.description: Build CI/CD pipelines: GitHub Actions for backend (test → build → deploy), firmware (compile → unit test → OTA package signing), frontend (lint → test → build → deploy to CDN), and ML model retraining pipeline.

pipelines.backend: pytest → docker build → ECR push → helm upgrade
pipelines.firmware: arm-none-eabi-gcc → unity tests → binary sign → S3 OTA bucket
pipelines.frontend: eslint → vitest → vite build → CloudFront invalidate
pipelines.ml: data_validate → train → evaluate → register_mlflow → deploy if MAPE improves

acceptance_criteria: Backend pipeline deploys to staging in < 8 minutes from merge to main
Firmware OTA package signed with HSM-backed key before S3 upload
ML pipeline blocks promotion if MAPE regresses > 2% vs production
Zero-downtime deployments via rolling update strategy on EKS
PagerDuty alert fires within 2 minutes of p99 latency exceeding 1 second

runbooks: vehicle_onboarding: ECU install checklist, IMEI registration, provisioning API call
eld_investigation: HOW to investigate driver ELD violations step-by-step

acceptance_criteria: Vehicle onboarding runbook enables a new field tech to provision a device in < 30 minutes
Telemetry outage triage tree covers all failure points from ECU to database

Data retention job tested in staging with confirmed deletion audit log

plan.description: Produce architecture documentation: system architecture diagram, data flow diagrams, MQTT topic schema reference, API contract, firmware ICD, and regulatory evidence index.

documents: firmware_ICD: message formats, CAN PGN mapping
regulatory_evidence_index: links all compliance artifacts to standards clauses
payload.format: Markdown in /docs, diagrams as Mermaid or draw.io

acceptance_criteria: C4 architecture diagrams cover all four levels (Context, Container, Component, Code for critical paths)
Regulatory evidence index maps every artifact to its governing standard clause
OpenAPI spec validates without errors in Swagger editor
Firmware ICD documents every MQTT payload field with type, unit, and range
Docs reviewed by developer and regulatory_specialist before release

flaws: MISRA C 'zero violations' in Steps 9 and 10 is unachievable in practice. FreeRTOS itself ships with documented MISRA deviations. The correct acceptance criterion is 'zero unjustified deviations' with a deviation log — not zero violations. Any CI gate enforcing zero will block the build permanently on the first third-party include.

ISO 26262 scope is misapplied. ISO 26262 governs E/E systems that can cause physical harm to vehicle occupants or road users. A read-only telematics ECU that does not actuate any vehicle system does not meet the 'controllability' threshold for ASIL assignment. Assigning ASIL A to 'HOS alerts' (an informational notification) and ASIL B to 'brake event detection' (which is passive logging, not brake control) misuses the standard and will fail a functional safety audit. The actual applicable standard for a data-logging device is ISO/SAE 21434 for cybersecurity, not ISO 26262.

AUTOSAR Classic BSW mapping in Steps 9 and 10 requires commercial AUTOSAR toolchains (EB tresos Studio, Vector DaVinci Configurator). These are $50k+ annual licenses. Acceptance criterion 'AUTOSAR compliance checked' is not a functional test — AUTOSAR conformance testing requires accredited test labs. Treating this as a firmware checkbox will fail any OEM integration audit.

ML training data bootstrapping problem is unresolved. Steps 14, 15, and 16 depend on 'labeled fleet data' and 'fleet data from Step 13,' but Step 13 is the ingestion infrastructure — not a data collection program. The product has no data before launch. The AUC >0.82 target on synthetic SHRP2 data does not validate real-world fleet performance. No warm-start strategy, no minimum fleet size for statistical validity, no model confidence floor before serving predictions to real operators.

SHRP2 naturalistic driving data access is non-trivial. It requires a formal data-sharing agreement with Virginia Tech Transportation Institute (VTTI) and IRB-equivalent review. Treating it as a passive dependency ('publicly available') is incorrect — it is licensed data with use restrictions.

Hardware design is entirely absent. The plan includes firmware (Step 9), hardware simulation (Step 8), and HIL testing (Step 23) but zero steps for PCB schematic, component selection, thermal design, EMC/EMI testing, or hardware design review. You cannot build firmware for undefined hardware.

FCC/IC/CE certification for the telematics ECU device is missing. Any device sold in the US that emits RF (LTE modem + GPS) requires FCC Part 15/Part 90 certification. This is a multi-month process with an accredited test lab. Without it, the device cannot be legally sold or installed. This is a hard stop before Step 28.

Cellular carrier certification is absent. Devices connecting to AT&T, Verizon, or T-Mobile LTE networks require carrier device certification (PTCRB/GCF) in addition to FCC approval. Fleet operators will demand certified devices. This can take 3-6 months and is not mentioned anywhere.

React Native Expo (Step 19) is incompatible with ELD compliance requirements. FMCSA mandates three data transfer methods including USB. Expo managed workflow does not support USB host/accessory mode, custom BLE service UUIDs required for ELD profile, or background location with the reliability required for continuous HOS tracking. This requires a bare React Native workflow with native modules — a significant architecture change.

ELD cryptographic signature private key storage on mobile (Step 19) has no security specification. 'ELD log sign-off generates cryptographic signature' without specifying Secure Enclave (iOS) / StrongBox (Android) usage means keys will likely land in AsyncStorage or app storage — readable by any process with device access. An FMCSA auditor or court discovery will invalidate signatures stored this way.

PostGIS is missing from the database design (Step 11). TimescaleDB does not natively support geospatial queries. The acceptance criterion 'geospatial queries return in <200ms on 1M rows' requires PostGIS with GIST spatial indexes. Without it, bounding-box queries will full-scan the telemetry table and fail the latency target at any meaningful scale.

MQTT topic structure enables cross-tenant enumeration. `fleet/{vehicle_id}/telemetry` with predictable vehicle IDs allows a subscriber with a valid broker connection to subscribe to `fleet/+/telemetry` and receive data across all fleets. Broker-level ACLs per tenant are not specified anywhere in the plan.

GDPR right to erasure vs. FMCSA 6-month retention is flagged as a documentation task (Step 25) but requires a concrete technical implementation decision. You cannot delete ELD records on GDPR request while simultaneously satisfying FMCSA retention. The legal position (FMCSA as a superseding federal obligation) must be codified in the privacy policy AND technically enforced (selective field suppression vs. full deletion). This is not just a lawyer task.

Vehicle provisioning flow has no dedicated design step. Step 31 references a 'vehicle onboarding runbook' and Step 21 mentions AWS IoT Core certificates, but no step designs the device provisioning API: IMEI registration, X.509 certificate issuance and push to device, fleet assignment, and first-telemetry validation. This is a critical operational gap — field technicians have no defined workflow.

UNECE R155 type approval process is misunderstood. R155 type approval is conducted by national type approval authorities (TÜV SÜD, DEKRA, etc.) on behalf of vehicle OEMs, not aftermarket telematics vendors. A telematics device supplier provides a CSMS and evidence package to the OEM, who applies for type approval. Step 6 and Step 24 treat this as the vendor's own certification — which is incorrect and will produce documentation that no OEM can use.

LTE PPP stack choice (Step 9) is outdated and fragile. Modern SIM7600/EC25 modems support ECM or RNDIS Ethernet emulation mode, which gives a standard network interface to the RTOS TCP/IP stack without implementing PPP. PPP adds dial-up-era complexity, higher CPU overhead, and fragile reconnection behavior that will cause store-and-forward data loss in poor signal conditions.

The plan has no V2X, fleet integrations (TMW, PeopleNet, Omnitracs), or ERP/dispatch system integration story. Competing against Samsara and Geotab without an open API and integration marketplace is a market positioning failure, not just a technical gap.

Load test in Step 28 targets '10,000 vehicles at 1Hz telemetry for 10 minutes' using k6. k6 is an HTTP load testing tool — it does not natively support MQTT protocol simulation at scale. 10,000 concurrent MQTT connections requires a purpose-built MQTT load generator (mqttx-cli, JMeter MQTT plugin, or custom tooling). Using k6 for this will produce misleading results.
suggestions: Replace 'MISRA C zero violations' with 'all MISRA C:2012 deviations documented with rationale, approved by safety engineer, total deviations < 50.' Use PC-lint Plus or LDRA for enforcement — not a CI regex.

Remove ISO 26262 from the telematics ECU scope unless the ECU will actuate vehicle systems. Replace with ISO/SAE 21434 (automotive cybersecurity), which is the correct standard for a connected telematics device. Retain the FMEA for failure mode analysis — just don't assign ASIL levels to passive logging functions.

Add a dedicated HARDWARE step between Steps 8 and 9: PCB schematic, component BOM with lead times, thermal design review, and EMC pre-scan. This unblocks firmware, HIL, and FCC certification in parallel. Without it, Steps 9, 23, and 28 have no validated hardware target.

Add FCC/IC/CE certification as an explicit step with a dependency on hardware design. Assign it between Steps 8 and 23. It is a critical path blocker for GA and must start as early as EVT hardware is stable.

For ML cold-start: define a 'shadow mode' launch where models run but do not surface predictions to users until a minimum 30-day dataset from 200+ vehicles is available per fleet. Specify synthetic pre-training accuracy thresholds separately from production accuracy thresholds.

Replace Expo with bare React Native from the start. Define required native modules: react-native-ble-plx (BLE ELD transfer), react-native-usb (USB transfer), react-native-keychain (Secure Enclave key storage), react-native-background-geolocation (continuous HOS tracking). Specify iOS minimum iOS 15 for CryptoKit Secure Enclave access.

Add PostGIS to Step 11's database design. Add GIST index on `telemetry(location)` where location is a PostGIS geography type. Restate geospatial query acceptance criterion explicitly as 'ST_Within bounding box query on PostGIS geography column with GIST index.'

Add an MQTT ACL design document as a deliverable in Step 12. Define per-tenant topic namespacing (e.g., `t/{tenant_id}/fleet/{vehicle_id}/telemetry`) and require broker-enforced ACL that rejects cross-tenant subscriptions. Test with a negative case: fleet A's credentials attempt to subscribe to fleet B's topic.

Split Step 24 into two steps: (a) FMCSA self-certification package (executable by the vendor), and (b) CSMS evidence package preparation for OEM partners pursuing UNECE R155 type approval. Clarify that your organization produces (b) as a vendor deliverable — OEMs carry the type approval burden.

Add an explicit MQTT load testing tool to Step 28. Use mqttx-cli (`mqttx bench pub -c 10000 -t 'fleet/{id}/telemetry' -im 1000`) or a custom Go MQTT publisher. Document the load test tool in the acceptance criteria so reviewers can reproduce the results.

Add a Step 33: FCC/IC/CE Certification and Carrier Approval. Dependencies: Step 8 (hardware sim), hardware design step. Deliverables: FCC grant of authorization, PTCRB certification report, carrier device approval letters. This is the hard gate before commercial deployment.
missing: Hardware PCB design step — schematics, BOM, component selection, thermal analysis, signal integrity review
FCC Part 15 / PTCRB / CE certification plan and timeline for the ECU device
Device provisioning API design — X.509 certificate lifecycle, IMEI registration, first-boot enrollment flow
PostGIS spatial extension in database design
MISRA C deviation management process (deviation log format, approval workflow, deviation budget)
ML warm-start and cold-start strategy — minimum data requirements before model serves live predictions
MQTT per-tenant ACL specification
OTA key ceremony procedure — HSM setup, key generation, key custody policy, rotation schedule, revocation procedure
V2X and third-party fleet management system integration API (TMW, McLeod, PeopleNet) — required for enterprise fleet operator adoption
Vehicle compatibility matrix — not all commercial vehicles expose J1939 PGNs; older vehicles may require OBD-II only fallback; some vehicles have non-standard CAN implementations
SIM card / cellular provider agreements and APN configuration management for deployed devices
GPS cold-start, TTFF (time to first fix) specification, and HDOP threshold policy (when to reject GPS readings as inaccurate)
FMCSA ELD Technical Standard version reference (current is v4.0) — acceptance criteria should cite specific document version
Carrier certification step (AT&T FirstNet for public safety, Verizon Network Access Requirements)
Step for GDPR data subject rights technical implementation — how deletion requests are processed when FMCSA retention creates a hold
security_risks: MQTT topic enumeration: `fleet/{vehicle_id}/telemetry` with sequential or guessable vehicle IDs allows a compromised or rogue device to subscribe to `fleet/+/telemetry` and harvest all fleet data. No broker ACL design is specified.

ELD cryptographic key on mobile device: Step 19 requires cryptographic signature of ELD logs but does not specify Secure Enclave (iOS) / Android StrongBox usage. Keys in app-accessible storage can be extracted from a rooted device, invalidating the tamper-evidence guarantee that regulators require.

WebSocket token refresh not addressed: Live map WebSocket connections are long-lived (hours). JWT expiry during an active session will either silently continue serving stale auth or abruptly disconnect fleet managers. Neither is specified, and the silent-continue case is a broken access control.

OTA rollback attack: Step 9 specifies ECDSA-256 signature verification for OTA updates but does not specify anti-rollback counters. A signed but older (vulnerable) firmware version is a valid ECDSA signature. Without a monotonic version counter in OTP memory, a downgrade attack restores known vulnerabilities.

CAN bus injection no authentication: Step 9 reads J1939 PGNs from CAN bus with no input validation or anomaly detection on the bus. A compromised OBD-II dongle or tampered CAN node could inject falsified fuel consumption, RPM, or mileage values that corrupt driver scoring and predictive maintenance models — directly undermining the product's core value proposition.

Driver GPS location continuous exposure: Step 25 lists GPS location as a collected data category, but the data model in Step 11 stores lat/lon at 10Hz. This is surveillance-grade location granularity. No data minimization strategy (e.g., location fuzzing at rest stops, geofenced location suppression near driver's home) is specified, creating CCPA/GDPR class action exposure.

SBOM not enforced in CI: Step 6 requires an SBOM for ECU firmware dependencies, but Step 22's firmware CI pipeline (`arm-none-eabi-gcc → unity tests → binary sign → S3`) has no SBOM generation or dependency vulnerability scanning step. The SBOM becomes a snapshot artifact, not a living supply chain control.
final_review.summary: This plan is broad, structured, and clearly authored by people who understand fleet telematics at a systems level. The dependency graph is coherent, the acceptance criteria are specific, and the regulatory awareness (FMCSA, UNECE R155) is above average for this type of plan. However, it has three fatal gaps that prevent a production score above 60 for a regulated hardware product. First, the hardware device — the physical ECU — has no design step, no PCB, no BOM, no FCC/CE certification plan, and no carrier approval process; firmware and HIL tests cannot proceed on undefined hardware, and the device cannot be legally sold without RF certification. Second, the ISO 26262 application is incorrect — assigning ASIL levels to passive data logging functions will fail any functional safety audit and may create liability exposure by implying safety claims the product cannot substantiate. Third, the ML models have a training data bootstrapping problem with no solution: the product needs real fleet data to produce useful predictions, but real fleet data only exists after the product is deployed and trusted. The plan assumes this problem doesn't exist. The MISRA C zero-violations criterion, AUTOSAR Classic without tooling budget, and Expo for an ELD-certified mobile app are individually fixable but collectively indicate the plan was written without scoping the embedded/regulatory implementation cost. Estimate these gaps add 4-8 months and $500k+ to the delivery timeline before the plan becomes executable as written.

flaws: MISRA C 'zero violations' in Steps 9 and 10 is unachievable in practice. FreeRTOS itself ships with documented MISRA deviations. The correct acceptance criterion is 'zero unjustified deviations' with a deviation log — not zero violations. Any CI gate enforcing zero will block the build permanently on the first third-party include.

ISO 26262 scope is misapplied. ISO 26262 governs E/E systems that can cause physical harm to vehicle occupants or road users. A read-only telematics ECU that does not actuate any vehicle system does not meet the 'controllability' threshold for ASIL assignment. Assigning ASIL A to 'HOS alerts' (an informational notification) and ASIL B to 'brake event detection' (which is passive logging, not brake control) misuses the standard and will fail a functional safety audit. The actual applicable standard for a data-logging device is ISO/SAE 21434 for cybersecurity, not ISO 26262.

AUTOSAR Classic BSW mapping in Steps 9 and 10 requires commercial AUTOSAR toolchains (EB tresos Studio, Vector DaVinci Configurator). These are $50k+ annual licenses. Acceptance criterion 'AUTOSAR compliance checked' is not a functional test — AUTOSAR conformance testing requires accredited test labs. Treating this as a firmware checkbox will fail any OEM integration audit.

ML training data bootstrapping problem is unresolved. Steps 14, 15, and 16 depend on 'labeled fleet data' and 'fleet data from Step 13,' but Step 13 is the ingestion infrastructure — not a data collection program. The product has no data before launch. The AUC >0.82 target on synthetic SHRP2 data does not validate real-world fleet performance. No warm-start strategy, no minimum fleet size for statistical validity, no model confidence floor before serving predictions to real operators.

SHRP2 naturalistic driving data access is non-trivial. It requires a formal data-sharing agreement with Virginia Tech Transportation Institute (VTTI) and IRB-equivalent review. Treating it as a passive dependency ('publicly available') is incorrect — it is licensed data with use restrictions.

Hardware design is entirely absent. The plan includes firmware (Step 9), hardware simulation (Step 8), and HIL testing (Step 23) but zero steps for PCB schematic, component selection, thermal design, EMC/EMI testing, or hardware design review. You cannot build firmware for undefined hardware.

FCC/IC/CE certification for the telematics ECU device is missing. Any device sold in the US that emits RF (LTE modem + GPS) requires FCC Part 15/Part 90 certification. This is a multi-month process with an accredited test lab. Without it, the device cannot be legally sold or installed. This is a hard stop before Step 28.

Cellular carrier certification is absent. Devices connecting to AT&T, Verizon, or T-Mobile LTE networks require carrier device certification (PTCRB/GCF) in addition to FCC approval. Fleet operators will demand certified devices. This can take 3-6 months and is not mentioned anywhere.

React Native Expo (Step 19) is incompatible with ELD compliance requirements. FMCSA mandates three data transfer methods including USB. Expo managed workflow does not support USB host/accessory mode, custom BLE service UUIDs required for ELD profile, or background location with the reliability required for continuous HOS tracking. This requires a bare React Native workflow with native modules — a significant architecture change.

ELD cryptographic signature private key storage on mobile (Step 19) has no security specification. 'ELD log sign-off generates cryptographic signature' without specifying Secure Enclave (iOS) / StrongBox (Android) usage means keys will likely land in AsyncStorage or app storage — readable by any process with device access. An FMCSA auditor or court discovery will invalidate signatures stored this way.

PostGIS is missing from the database design (Step 11). TimescaleDB does not natively support geospatial queries. The acceptance criterion 'geospatial queries return in <200ms on 1M rows' requires PostGIS with GIST spatial indexes. Without it, bounding-box queries will full-scan the telemetry table and fail the latency target at any meaningful scale.

MQTT topic structure enables cross-tenant enumeration. `fleet/{vehicle_id}/telemetry` with predictable vehicle IDs allows a subscriber with a valid broker connection to subscribe to `fleet/+/telemetry` and receive data across all fleets. Broker-level ACLs per tenant are not specified anywhere in the plan.

GDPR right to erasure vs. FMCSA 6-month retention is flagged as a documentation task (Step 25) but requires a concrete technical implementation decision. You cannot delete ELD records on GDPR request while simultaneously satisfying FMCSA retention. The legal position (FMCSA as a superseding federal obligation) must be codified in the privacy policy AND technically enforced (selective field suppression vs. full deletion). This is not just a lawyer task.

Vehicle provisioning flow has no dedicated design step. Step 31 references a 'vehicle onboarding runbook' and Step 21 mentions AWS IoT Core certificates, but no step designs the device provisioning API: IMEI registration, X.509 certificate issuance and push to device, fleet assignment, and first-telemetry validation. This is a critical operational gap — field technicians have no defined workflow.

UNECE R155 type approval process is misunderstood. R155 type approval is conducted by national type approval authorities (TÜV SÜD, DEKRA, etc.) on behalf of vehicle OEMs, not aftermarket telematics vendors. A telematics device supplier provides a CSMS and evidence package to the OEM, who applies for type approval. Step 6 and Step 24 treat this as the vendor's own certification — which is incorrect and will produce documentation that no OEM can use.

LTE PPP stack choice (Step 9) is outdated and fragile. Modern SIM7600/EC25 modems support ECM or RNDIS Ethernet emulation mode, which gives a standard network interface to the RTOS TCP/IP stack without implementing PPP. PPP adds dial-up-era complexity, higher CPU overhead, and fragile reconnection behavior that will cause store-and-forward data loss in poor signal conditions.

The plan has no V2X, fleet integrations (TMW, PeopleNet, Omnitracs), or ERP/dispatch system integration story. Competing against Samsara and Geotab without an open API and integration marketplace is a market positioning failure, not just a technical gap.

Load test in Step 28 targets '10,000 vehicles at 1Hz telemetry for 10 minutes' using k6. k6 is an HTTP load testing tool — it does not natively support MQTT protocol simulation at scale. 10,000 concurrent MQTT connections requires a purpose-built MQTT load generator (mqttx-cli, JMeter MQTT plugin, or custom tooling). Using k6 for this will produce misleading results.
suggestions: Replace 'MISRA C zero violations' with 'all MISRA C:2012 deviations documented with rationale, approved by safety engineer, total deviations < 50.' Use PC-lint Plus or LDRA for enforcement — not a CI regex.

Remove ISO 26262 from the telematics ECU scope unless the ECU will actuate vehicle systems. Replace with ISO/SAE 21434 (automotive cybersecurity), which is the correct standard for a connected telematics device. Retain the FMEA for failure mode analysis — just don't assign ASIL levels to passive logging functions.

Add a dedicated HARDWARE step between Steps 8 and 9: PCB schematic, component BOM with lead times, thermal design review, and EMC pre-scan. This unblocks firmware, HIL, and FCC certification in parallel. Without it, Steps 9, 23, and 28 have no validated hardware target.

Add FCC/IC/CE certification as an explicit step with a dependency on hardware design. Assign it between Steps 8 and 23. It is a critical path blocker for GA and must start as early as EVT hardware is stable.

For ML cold-start: define a 'shadow mode' launch where models run but do not surface predictions to users until a minimum 30-day dataset from 200+ vehicles is available per fleet. Specify synthetic pre-training accuracy thresholds separately from production accuracy thresholds.

Replace Expo with bare React Native from the start. Define required native modules: react-native-ble-plx (BLE ELD transfer), react-native-usb (USB transfer), react-native-keychain (Secure Enclave key storage), react-native-background-geolocation (continuous HOS tracking). Specify iOS minimum iOS 15 for CryptoKit Secure Enclave access.

Add PostGIS to Step 11's database design. Add GIST index on `telemetry(location)` where location is a PostGIS geography type. Restate geospatial query acceptance criterion explicitly as 'ST_Within bounding box query on PostGIS geography column with GIST index.'

Add an MQTT ACL design document as a deliverable in Step 12. Define per-tenant topic namespacing (e.g., `t/{tenant_id}/fleet/{vehicle_id}/telemetry`) and require broker-enforced ACL that rejects cross-tenant subscriptions. Test with a negative case: fleet A's credentials attempt to subscribe to fleet B's topic.

Split Step 24 into two steps: (a) FMCSA self-certification package (executable by the vendor), and (b) CSMS evidence package preparation for OEM partners pursuing UNECE R155 type approval. Clarify that your organization produces (b) as a vendor deliverable — OEMs carry the type approval burden.

Add an explicit MQTT load testing tool to Step 28. Use mqttx-cli (`mqttx bench pub -c 10000 -t 'fleet/{id}/telemetry' -im 1000`) or a custom Go MQTT publisher. Document the load test tool in the acceptance criteria so reviewers can reproduce the results.

Add a Step 33: FCC/IC/CE Certification and Carrier Approval. Dependencies: Step 8 (hardware sim), hardware design step. Deliverables: FCC grant of authorization, PTCRB certification report, carrier device approval letters. This is the hard gate before commercial deployment.
missing: Hardware PCB design step — schematics, BOM, component selection, thermal analysis, signal integrity review
FCC Part 15 / PTCRB / CE certification plan and timeline for the ECU device
Device provisioning API design — X.509 certificate lifecycle, IMEI registration, first-boot enrollment flow
PostGIS spatial extension in database design
MISRA C deviation management process (deviation log format, approval workflow, deviation budget)
ML warm-start and cold-start strategy — minimum data requirements before model serves live predictions
MQTT per-tenant ACL specification
OTA key ceremony procedure — HSM setup, key generation, key custody policy, rotation schedule, revocation procedure
V2X and third-party fleet management system integration API (TMW, McLeod, PeopleNet) — required for enterprise fleet operator adoption
Vehicle compatibility matrix — not all commercial vehicles expose J1939 PGNs; older vehicles may require OBD-II only fallback; some vehicles have non-standard CAN implementations
SIM card / cellular provider agreements and APN configuration management for deployed devices
GPS cold-start, TTFF (time to first fix) specification, and HDOP threshold policy (when to reject GPS readings as inaccurate)
FMCSA ELD Technical Standard version reference (current is v4.0) — acceptance criteria should cite specific document version
Carrier certification step (AT&T FirstNet for public safety, Verizon Network Access Requirements)
Step for GDPR data subject rights technical implementation — how deletion requests are processed when FMCSA retention creates a hold
